cover.title: THE MOUNTAIN
cover.subtitle: The one who holds everyone up, and rarely lets anyone hold them.
cover.tagline: You carry so much alone that people forget you need somewhere to lean too.

profile_scores.caption: High Stability · High Care, Low Expression. Always holding others up, rarely leaning on anyone or showing it

short_summary: You love by becoming someone others can lean on. Steady, present, and hard to shake.
The loneliness you've felt before wasn't because you were alone. It was because you were always the one holding on.
What you're still learning isn't how to be stronger. It's how to let someone support you too.

full_report.headline: THE MOUNTAIN
full_report.intro: You love by becoming steady for the people you care about, often before they even ask. This report follows why you keep becoming the one who holds everything together, what that costs you, and what changes when care finally goes both ways.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You're not someone who leans on others easily.
When something hard happens, you handle it yourself first. When a problem comes up, you think it through alone before saying a word. You try not to bother people if you can help it.
So people think you're strong. They think you're dependable. They think you're the mature one.
That is only half the story. You need someone too. You've just gotten used to keeping that part to yourself.
Love brings out the same instinct. When they're struggling, you stay close. When they're shaking, you hold them steady. When they're falling apart, you catch them.
But when you're the one who's shaking, you say nothing.
So this happens: people have no idea how much you're actually carrying.
chapters.profile_note: High Stability · High Care, Low Expression. Always holding others up, rarely leaning on anyone or showing it

chapters.title: The Pattern You Keep Repeating
body: This pattern may be older than you realize.
Someone struggles. You're naturally there beside them. They may not even have to ask. It just happens. And slowly, they get more comfortable leaning on you, while you get more used to carrying it.
That's where the problem can start. They begin to think you're "fine." But sometimes, you were only trying to look fine. That gap matters.
They think you don't need anything. You never say that you do.
So the longer the relationship goes on, the easier it is to feel lonely inside it.
chapters.highlight: The loneliness isn't always about being alone. Sometimes it comes from always being the one who holds everything together.

chart.title: THE UNSEEN WEIGHT CURVE
chart.description: As the relationship goes on, responsibility keeps growing, while asking for help keeps shrinking.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.belief: I'm just someone who's comfortable being alone.
items.truth:
Maybe not.

Being alone may not feel comfortable so much as familiar. And familiar and comfortable aren't always the same thing.
items.truth_short: Being alone may feel familiar, but familiar and comfortable aren't the same thing.

items.belief: My problems are mine to solve.
items.truth:
Think about it.

If someone you cared about told you they were struggling, you'd help them. You deserve that same chance to be supported.
items.truth_short: You would help someone you cared about. You don't have to be the exception.

items.belief: Showing that I'm struggling is a burden to others.
items.truth:
Asking for help doesn't make you a burden.

Keeping everything to yourself can leave the other person with no clear way to show up for you. If someone's going to care for you, they need a chance to know how you're actually doing.
items.truth_short: Asking for help isn't a burden. It gives someone a chance to show up for you.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: You may find yourself drawn to people who clearly need something to lean on. Someone a little unsteady. Someone who feels safer when you're nearby.
It makes sense. You're good at listening, taking things in, and helping someone feel steady.
But over time, something can feel off. They're receiving help from you, while your own need for support stays mostly unseen.
chapters.highlight: Someone who trusts you uses your shoulder. Someone who loves you also checks whether that shoulder is getting heavy.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't to get even stronger. You're already strong enough.
What changes things is letting the parts that need care become visible too.
Say it when something's hard. Ask for what you need. Let yourself receive help.
It can take more courage than it sounds like it should.
That's how care starts moving in both directions.
chapters.action_sentence: That's how care starts moving in both directions.

chapters.title: Where You Go From Here
body: You already know how to take responsibility. You already know how to protect someone. You already know how to stay until the end.
The next step is learning how to hold on together.
Not carrying everything alone, but letting the other person take part in what keeps the relationship steady.
The strongest relationships make room for two people to carry what matters.
chapters.transition_note: The strongest relationships make room for two people to carry what matters.

chapters.title: The Types You Keep Falling For

items.type_title: The Person Who Looks Strong But Still Needs Support
items.description: They seem capable. They take care of things. People rely on them. But when life gets heavy, they may turn to you to hold more than your share.

items.type_title: The Person Who Needs Somewhere To Lean
items.description: Something goes wrong, and you're the first person they call. A difficult day. A family problem. A decision they don't want to make alone. At first, being needed feels meaningful. But slowly, you realize they only know how to stand when you're holding part of the weight.

items.type_title: Someone Who Struggles to Manage Alone
items.description: They struggle to decide things or keep them organized. So you step in. At some point, you may notice you're carrying far more than feels fair.

items.type_title: Someone Who Says Thank You But Never Changes
items.description: They appreciate you. They mean it. But appreciation alone doesn't make a relationship healthier. You keep giving. They keep thanking you. And nothing actually changes.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: These signs can be easy to miss when you're used to making room for the other person's needs first.

signal_strength.label: You hear about their problems often, but you barely mention your own
They ask you for help far more often than you ask them
Their problems have a place on your schedule, but no one knows about yours
The harder things get, the less you say about what you're carrying

chapters.core_signal: They may believe you're fine because that's the version you've learned to show them.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like for You

checklist.title: Asking for help isn't strange.
checklist.description: You don't have to read the room. You don't have to feel sorry about it. You just say it.

checklist.title: You don't have to be strong all the time.
checklist.description: Some days you can be exhausted. You can make mistakes. You can lean on someone.

checklist.title: When problems come up, you work on them together.
checklist.description: One person doesn't automatically become the fixer. You look at the problem together and decide what to do together.

checklist.title: The care goes both ways.
checklist.description: You're not the only one paying attention. They notice when you need care too.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you:
You may feel pulled toward someone who has trouble carrying their own problems.

At first, helping can feel a lot like love. Being needed can feel meaningful.

But if responsibility keeps moving in only one direction, you can slowly stop feeling like a partner and start feeling like the fixer.
sections.who_steadies_you:
A steadier match is someone who can speak clearly about what they need and make room for what you need too. They don't leave every hard thing with you. They ask, listen, and take responsibility for their part.

Around someone like this, support doesn't have to be earned by carrying everything first.
sections.same_pattern_repeats:
You may still feel drawn to someone who needs your steadiness. That attraction isn't the problem by itself.

What matters is whether they also make room for your needs, take responsibility for their own choices, and notice when the weight stops feeling shared.
sections.next_signs:
This time, the signs may be easier to notice earlier.

Pay attention to what happens when you mention a hard day, ask for support, or let someone see that you don't have everything handled.

The important part isn't one perfect response. It's whether care and responsibility begin moving both ways.
sections.try_this:
Try asking for one small, honest thing you genuinely need.

It could be a call, help with a plan, or company on a difficult day.

Then notice what follows over time. Do they remember? Do they make room? Can they support you without turning your need into a problem?

You already know how to help someone else. This is about learning whether support can feel mutual.
sections.final_line: You don't need proof from one moment. You need a pattern that lets you lean too.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to support someone else. What's left is learning to let care move both ways.
action_items: This week, ask for one small favor instead of handling everything yourself.
When something feels hard, say it before you have already solved it alone.
Notice whether the other person makes room for your needs, not only their own.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning how to be someone else's support. The next report covers why you keep ending up in the role of the one who holds on, and how you relearn what it means to lean on someone.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
